cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to figure out the answer to the question about why people come to Pompeii each year. The question is asking for the reason that large numbers of tourists visit, and the choices are A through D. First, I'll look back at the passage provided to see what information is relevant. The passage starts by mentioning that thousands of tourists visit Pompeii every year and lists what they see: the stadium and theaters, shops, and restaurants. However, it's emphasized that they don't see the people because no one has lived there for 2000 years. Then the passage details the history of Pompeii, explaining how it was destroyed by the eruption of Mt. Vesuvius in 79 AD. The city was buried under ash and stones. Later, it was uncovered by Giuseppe in 1861, and many artifacts were found that tell about the daily lives of Pompeiians, like glasses and jars with blue color (wine), bread pans in bakeries, and items showing women used eye makeup. The passage also mentions that scientists are still digging and making discoveries that attract tourists. Now, the question is about why people come. The options are to visit the volcano (A), to shop and eat (B), to watch sports and plays (C), or to see how Pompeiians lived (D). Looking at the passage, the first paragraph says tourists see the stadium and theaters, shops and restaurants, but that's the physical structures and not the people. However, the rest of the passage explains that the reason for their interest is the historical remains and artifacts that give insight into the lives of the people who lived there. The author points out that the everyday objects found in Pompeii tell us a lot about the people, like their preference for wine, bread, and makeup. The last paragraph also mentions that the work continues and discoveries draw tourists. So even though the first part mentions the physical buildings (stadium, theaters, shops), the main reason given in the passage for the tourists coming is actually to see the historical remnants that reveal how the people lived, not just to visit the volcano or to use the shops/restaurants (which are now gone, since no one lives
